metadata.title: Summary vs. Synthesis: The Move No Source Can Make For You

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: So synthesis is the move no single source can make: reading Berners-Lee, Poore, and Willett in relation so one becomes a premise, another sharpens the claim, and the gap between them becomes my argument. I want to practice the turn. Have Claude (1) show the summary version of three sources side by side with the synthesis version, (2) mark exactly where a source shifts from being reported to being made load-bearing for a new claim, and (3) name the claim that emerges only from the three together.

props.command: So synthesis is the move no single source can make: reading Berners-Lee, Poore, and Willett in relation so one becomes a premise, another sharpens the claim, and the gap between them becomes my argument. I want to practice the turn. Have Claude (1) show the summary version of three sources side by side with the synthesis version, (2) mark exactly where a source shifts from being reported to being made load-bearing for a new claim, and (3) name the claim that emerges only from the three together.
props.segment: Summary vs. Synthesis: The Move No

beats.narration_text: You read ten sources carefully. You cite all of them correctly. You hand in the paper. And you have not made a single argument.

card.sub: the problem is not the sources

beats.narration_text: The problem is not your sources. It is what you did with them. Most first drafts of argumentative research papers are not arguments. They are summaries arranged in order.
beats.scene_description: a student's annotated draft manuscript, dense with underlines and marginal notes, pinned to newsprint ground — push-in toward the annotated lines
beats.image_prompt: B02, printed student essay with handwritten marginal annotations and underlining, multiple sticky notes, placed flat on aged newsprint surface, desaturated editorial collage treatment, overhead angle, flat print lighting

beats.narration_text: What is the difference between summarizing sources and synthesizing them — and why does the second require a move no single source can make for you?

card.copy: What is the difference between summarizing and synthesizing?
card.sub: and why does synthesis require a move no source can make for you?

beats.narration_text: Summary looks right. It reports each source accurately. The citations are real. The sentences are true. A reader who checked every claim would find nothing wrong. And yet nothing has been argued.

production_viz.label: three summary sentences, one per source
production_viz.mechanic: three sentence-bars appear one at a time, each labeled with a citation chip (Berners-Lee 2018 / Willett 2019 / Poore 2018) in CRIMSON; no connecting arrow between them
production_viz.note: CRIMSON = summary / the gap. Each bar is a stand-alone report.

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] three summary sentences — separate, isolated, crimson

beats.narration_text: Here is the summary version. Three sources. Three sentences. Berners-Lee argues food production is sufficient. Willett argues for plant-based diets. Poore documents which food systems do the most damage.

production_viz.label: the summary paragraph rendered as three isolated blocks
production_viz.mechanic: three rectangular blocks slide in, each carrying a short paraphrase and a citation chip; blocks are separate, no arrows connecting them; CRIMSON chips
production_viz.note: illustrative — the exact phrasing from the chapter's summary example, abbreviated for screen

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] the three-sentence summary laid out as three isolated blocks

beats.narration_text: A reader who read all three sources learns nothing new from those three sentences. The citations are load-bearing. But the argument is not there. The sources are carrying each other — not you.

production_viz.label: three isolated source blocks — no writer arrow
production_viz.mechanic: the three crimson-labeled blocks from B05 remain; a dotted placeholder arc appears between them, then fades — the place where an argument would go, shown as absent
production_viz.note: the missing arrow is the point — not drawn, just the ghost of where it should be

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] the missing argument arrow — ghost placeholder

beats.narration_text: Synthesis does something different. It reads the sources in relation to each other — asking not what each source says, but what they say together that none of them said alone.

card.copy: Reading sources in relation to each other
card.sub: not what each says — what they say together

beats.new_visual_element: [SECTION CARD] the mechanism — reading in relation

beats.narration_text: The synthesis version uses the same three sources. But now Berners-Lee's finding becomes a premise — if production is already sufficient, the problem is not making more food. It is distribution.

production_viz.label: Berners-Lee block becomes a premise; an arrow points toward a claim
production_viz.mechanic: the Berners-Lee block slides to the left anchor position; an arrow grows rightward toward a central claim box that reads 'distribution problem'; block is now INK-colored (synthesis)
production_viz.note: INK = synthesis / the argument emerging. Contrast with CRIMSON blocks from B04-B06.

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] Berners-Lee as premise — arrow toward distribution claim

beats.narration_text: Poore and Nemecek's data now sharpens that claim — the highest-impact food systems serve the richest consumers. And EAT-Lancet's recommendations address consumption, but largely leave the distribution question untouched.

production_viz.label: all three sources converge on the synthesis claim
production_viz.mechanic: Poore and Willett blocks slide into position on the left; arrows from all three converge on the central claim box — a fourth element none of them contained individually; all in INK
production_viz.note: the fourth element — the central claim — is the writer's contribution

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] three sources — three arrows — one new claim at center

beats.narration_text: That claim — that the food-sustainability literature has under-addressed the distribution dimension — is not in any of the three sources. It is the argument the writer made by reading them against each other.
beats.scene_description: the cover of the Lancet EAT-Lancet issue (January 2019), desaturated editorial treatment, pinned to newsprint ground — slow push toward the title text
beats.image_prompt: B10, The Lancet journal cover January 2019 EAT-Lancet Commission issue, desaturated flat print editorial collage treatment, pinned to aged newsprint ground, overhead flat lighting
beats.new_visual_element: [STILL] EAT-Lancet Lancet cover — the literature the synthesis reads

beats.narration_text: This is what the chapter means by synthesis. The argument lives between sources, not inside any of them. You are the one connecting them — and the connection is the argument.

production_viz.label: side-by-side: summary (crimson, isolated) vs synthesis (ink, connected)
production_viz.mechanic: screen splits: left side shows the three CRIMSON isolated blocks from the summary; right side shows the three INK blocks with arrows converging on a central claim; a vertical dividing line between them; label chips SUMMARY and SYNTHESIS
production_viz.note: the core visual object — the side-by-side with the fourth element visible only on the right

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] side-by-side: SUMMARY (isolated, crimson) vs SYNTHESIS (connected, ink + arrow)

beats.narration_text: Here is the summary paragraph from the chapter, as written. Three sentences. Each one accurate. But the paragraph as a whole does not argue anything — it describes a set of findings.

document.quote: Berners-Lee et al. (2018) argue that global food production is currently sufficient. Willett et al. (2019) propose dietary shifts. Poore and Nemecek (2018) document environmental footprints.
document.attribution: summary version — each sentence reports one source

beats.new_visual_element: [DOCUMENT] the summary paragraph — gold highlight on 'reports one source'

beats.narration_text: Now watch the synthesis version. Production is sufficient — so the problem is distribution. EAT-Lancet addresses consumption but leaves distribution largely untouched. Poore sharpens that: the highest-impact systems serve the richest consumers. One argument. Four elements. Three sources and the writer's move.

production_viz.label: the synthesis paragraph — animated as sentences rearrange and claim appears
production_viz.mechanic: three source-blocks animate in, then arrows connect them; a fourth element (the claim bar, in bold INK) rises above them — the argument that none of the three sources contains individually; CRIMSON highlight fades as INK argument appears
production_viz.note: the morph: this is the central animation — summary arrangement transforms into synthesis arrangement with the claim appearing at the top

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] the morph — three blocks rearrange, argument claim rises above them

beats.narration_text: Two diagnostic questions. First: if you remove all the citations, is there still an argument? If yes, the citations are evidence. If no, you are describing the sources, not arguing.

production_viz.label: diagnostic question 1 — remove citations, is there still an argument?
production_viz.mechanic: a paragraph block with citation chips; the chips fade out one by one; the remaining text either holds as an argument (INK, stays solid) or dissolves to nothing (CRIMSON, fades); two outcomes shown side by side
production_viz.note: illustrative diagnostic — not tied to any specific text

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] diagnostic 1 — citation chips fade, argument either holds or vanishes

beats.narration_text: Second: does this paragraph say something that no single source also says? If yes, that is likely synthesis. If no, you may be paraphrasing one source at a time and calling it an argument.

production_viz.label: diagnostic question 2 — does the paragraph say something no single source says?
production_viz.mechanic: three source blocks; a 'writer's claim' box appears above; arrows from sources to claim animate; label reads 'the fourth element'; source blocks are individually checked against the claim — none contains it alone
production_viz.note: the fourth element — the claim arrow points to something none of the three sources individually contains

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] diagnostic 2 — the fourth element above the three sources

beats.narration_text: Summary reports what each source says. Synthesis reads sources in relation to each other — and produces an interpretation none of them produced individually. The connection is yours to make. That is the argument.

card.copy: Synthesis is the argument no source makes for you.
card.sub: does your paragraph say something no single source also says?

beats.new_visual_element: [ENDCARD] synthesis is the argument no source makes for you

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Here's what the body just demonstrated. This is what the chapter means by synthesis. The argument lives between sources, not inside any of them. You are the one connecting them — and the connection is the argument. Here is the summary paragraph from the chapter, as written. Three sentences. Each one accurate. But the paragraph as a whole does not argue anything — it describes a set of findings.

props.artifactHeading: Summary vs. Synthesis: The Move No Source Can
artifactLines: The synthesis version uses the same three sources.
This is what the chapter means by synthesis.
Summary reports what each source says.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Summary vs. Synthesis: The Move No Source Can Make For You] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
props.segment: Summary vs. Synthesis: The Move No
